title: Linux & Python Reliability Toolkit
whatIProved: I think like an operator, not someone who follows tutorials. Linux is asked in every DevOps interview.
description: A Python-based system monitor that detects failed systemd services, parses journalctl logs for errors, auto-restarts failing services, and fires Slack/email alerts when thresholds are breached. Wrapped as a systemd service with structured JSON logging.
highlights: Structured JSON logging with the Python logging module
Webhook alerting on threshold breach
Systemd integration with Restart=on-failure
3 documented incident reports with root cause analysis

title: Bug Tracker — Containerized Stack
whatIProved: I understand how containers communicate with each other — asked in every DevOps and cloud interview without exception.
description: A FastAPI bug/incident tracker containerized with Docker and orchestrated via Docker Compose. Multi-stage builds, custom bridge networking, Nginx reverse proxy, non-root user execution, resource limits, graceful shutdown handling, and blue-green scaling with docker-compose scale.
highlights: Multi-stage build reduced image size below 150MB
Custom bridge network with DNS-based service discovery
Nginx reverse proxy with upstream load balancing
4 documented failure scenarios with debugging traces

title: AWS Infrastructure Provisioner
whatIProved: I can provision real cloud infrastructure from code and configure it automatically — the biggest skill gap between juniors and mid-level DevOps.
description: End-to-end AWS infrastructure provisioned via Terraform: VPC with public/private subnets, NAT Gateway, S3 remote state with DynamoDB locking, least-privilege IAM, bastion host. Configured via Ansible with role-based playbooks, idempotency verified on second run, and CloudWatch log shipping.
highlights: S3 remote state backend + DynamoDB state locking
Least-privilege IAM role (S3 read + CloudWatch write only)
Ansible roles verified idempotent on second run
Terraform drift detection documented as incident report

title: Secure CI/CD Pipeline
whatIProved: I can automate deployment from code commit to live server, with security gates and zero-downtime rollout.
description: GitHub Actions pipeline: lint → test → build → Trivy scan → push to ECR → deploy to EC2 → ALB health check → auto-rollback. Blue/green deployment via ALB target group switching. Pipeline blocks on CRITICAL CVEs.
highlights: Trivy blocks pipeline on CRITICAL CVEs
Auto-rollback on health check failure
Blue/green via ALB target group swap
Zero-downtime deployment verified end-to-end

title: Kubernetes + War Room Stack
whatIProved: Operational maturity — the ability to walk through a real incident from symptoms to prevention. This separates 8 LPA candidates from 12 LPA candidates.
description: Full Kubernetes deployment of the Bug Tracker: Deployment, Service, Ingress, HPA, PDB, NetworkPolicy, PersistentVolumeClaim, RBAC. Prometheus + Grafana + AlertManager via Helm. 4 chaos scenarios executed with RED/USE metric analysis and structured incident reports.
highlights: 4 chaos scenarios: pod crash, CPU spike, network partition, node drain
HPA targeting 60% CPU, min 1 / max 5 pods
NetworkPolicy restricting API-to-DB only
Structured incident reports: Hypothesis → Symptoms → Root Cause → Prevention

title: MLOps Production Pipeline
whatIProved: I can deploy and manage an ML model like a production service — with versioning, monitoring, rollback, and cost awareness.
description: FastAPI serving a Hugging Face sentiment model with P99 latency monitoring, blue/green rollout in Kubernetes, automatic rollback on latency spike, model versioning via S3, and cost estimation per instance type.
highlights: Model artifacts versioned in S3, loaded on startup
P99 latency exposed as Prometheus histogram
Auto-rollback triggers if P99 exceeds 3x baseline
Cost estimation documented per request volume

title: LLM / RAG Infrastructure
whatIProved: I understand the business of AI — cost control, rate limiting, secret management — not just the hype.
description: RAG pipeline: document chunking → embedding → ChromaDB → LLM query with source citation. Per-request cost tracking, Redis caching (cache hit eliminates LLM cost), rate limiting middleware (429 with retry-after), API key rotation without pod restart.
highlights: Per-query cost tracked: input + output tokens × price
Redis cache eliminates LLM cost on repeated queries
Rate limiter returns clean 429 with retry-after header
API key rotation plan documented without downtime

title: AI-Augmented SRE Dashboard
whatIProved: I understand where AI helps and where it shouldn't be trusted. I design safe systems — which makes me sound senior even as a junior.
description: AlertManager webhook receiver that fetches related logs, builds an LLM prompt, returns structured JSON root-cause analysis with a confidence score (HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW), and sends a Slack message with a 'Do NOT act without verification' warning. Human approval gate before any action.
highlights: Confidence scoring: HIGH if matches known runbook, LOW if no context
Every AI output logged to S3 as immutable audit trail
Human approval gate — nothing automated without explicit confirm
Limitations section in README: what the AI gets wrong
